title: Microscopic Theory of the Phonon Thermal Hall Effect in Chiral Mott Insulators
abstract: The thermal Hall effect (THE) probes charge-neutral excitations in insulators, where the charge gap blocks electronic transport. Recently, phonons have been shown to induce a THE comparable in magnitude to the spin contribution, underscoring their critical role in thermal transport. Here, we develop a microscopic theory of the phonon thermal Hall effect (PTHE) in chiral Mott insulators. First, we derive the exact analytic form of the effective Raman interaction in half-filled Mott insulators, showing that its strength is directly proportional to the scalar spin chirality. Next, we demonstrate the intrinsic PTHE explicitly on the kagome lattice. Crucially, our formulation reveals a temperature-dependent crossover in the transport behavior under isotopic substitution. Using this result, we establish a scaling law that quantitatively separates the phonon contribution to the THE from other background signals. Our results not only provide the first fully microscopic derivation of the PTHE, but also establish a definitive experimental standard for isolating microscopic heat carriers in chiral Mott insulators.
summary: This work establishes a fully microscopic theory for the Phonon Thermal Hall Effect in chiral Mott insulators. By deriving the effect from an emergent gauge field linked to spin chirality, the authors propose a definitive experimental protocol using isotopic substitution. This allows researchers to cleanly separate the phonon contribution to thermal transport from other background electronic signals.
main_problem: The paper develops a microscopic theory for the Phonon Thermal Hall Effect (PTHE) in chiral Mott insulators, aiming to provide a definitive method to isolate this phonon contribution.
main_result: The PTHE is shown to be governed by an emergent gauge field, which is proportional to the scalar spin chirality, and its signal can be quantitatively separated using isotopic substitution and a derived scaling law.
method: The theory employs advanced many-body techniques, including inverse Schrieffer-Wolff perturbation theory, Born-Oppenheimer approximations, and the calculation of Abelian phonon Berry curvature using projector formalisms.
model_or_system: The study focuses on chiral Mott insulators, modeled using the double exchange Hamiltonian at half-filling, and specifically investigates the 2D kagome lattice.
key_observables:
Phonon thermal Hall conductivity ($\kappa_{ph}^{\mu
u}$), emergent gauge field ($F_{em}^{ab}$), and the temperature/mass dependence of $\kappa_{ph}^{\mu
u}$.
important_parameters: Scalar spin chirality ($\chi_{ijk}$), temperature ($T$), and isotopic mass ratio ($M_r$).
main_assumptions: The calculation assumes hopping amplitudes depend only on inter-atomic displacement, and the method relies on the Born-Oppenheimer approximation for separating electron and nuclear dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the intrinsic PTHE in the kagome model, showing band structure changes and the temperature dependence of $\kappa_{ph}^{xy}(T)$, and demonstrate the isotope effect where $\kappa_{ph}^{xy}$ changes dependence on $M_r$ at low vs. high temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from deriving the effective lattice Hamiltonian via BO approximation, calculating the emergent gauge field via perturbation theory, and finally establishing the experimental protocol using isotopic substitution and scaling laws to isolate the pure PTHE signal.
why_interesting: The rigorous derivation of the PTHE links lattice vibrations (phonons) directly to electronic magnetic order (spin chirality) via emergent gauge fields, providing a powerful new diagnostic tool for strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Intrinsic phonon thermal Hall effect in the kagome Mott insulator. (a) Kagome lattice geometry with a canted antiferromagnetic order. The 10◦out-of-plane cant- ing yields a uniform scalar spin chirality χ = 0.055. (b) Phonon band structure. Solid red curves denote the unper- turbed dispersion (χ = 0); dashed blue curves incorporate the emergent gauge field (t/J = 0.3, γ = 4 ˚A−1). Calculations assume an interatomic spacing 4 ˚A, nuclear mass 10−25 kg, and longitudinal/transverse spring constants 20 N/m and 10 N/m, respectively. (c) Momentum-resolved thermal Hall con- ductivity integrand evaluated at T = 100 K. (d) Macroscopic thermal Hall conductivity κph xy as a function of...

FIG. 2. Isotope effect and mass scaling. (a) Absolute value of thermal Hall conductivity as a function of the rela- tive isotopic mass Mr. At low temperatures (T = 30 K, green dots, left axis), heavier masses enhance the transport, whereas in the high-temperature limit (orange dots, right axis), the macroscopic signal is strictly suppressed. The solid orange line indicates a power-law fit κph xy ∝M −0.877 r . (b) Temper- ature dependence of |κph xy| for Mr = 1 (magenta), 1.5 (red), and 2 (cyan). The dotted lines are fits to the ansatz [Eq. (15)] over the range 43 K ≤T ≤300 K.
